title: Babel
author: R.F. Kuang
review: The most enthralling book I've read all year. A literary marvel that made learning other languages sound magic, not just due to the fantasy nature of the story. The characters were vivid and unique, the descriptions of university echoed my own experience of wonder. A magical read.

title: Stoner
author: John  Williams
review:
Literature reflects upon experiences of life; real or imagined. Stoner is as depressing as all life journey's are, filled with regrets, love, friendships, foes, growth, disappointment and eventually death.

I feel this is a really important book for thanatophobes like myself, because it frames death with the perspective of a life lived.

title: Cobalt Red: How the Blood of the Congo Powers Our Lives
author: Siddharth Kara
review: A must read for anyone in tech, anyone in the sciences, anyone with a heart.

title: Dune (Dune, #1)
author: Frank Herbert
review:
A classic for good reasons.

Even more enjoyable on a second read.

title: To Kill a Mockingbird
author: Harper Lee
review:
This love captures the true expression of love for one's fellow man without prejudice for lifestyle, creed or colour. The first half I thought, at first, was well written if a bit dull. By the second half I was deeply emotionally invested. I'm so glad I read this, I can easily say this is one of my favourite books.

EDIT: My 2nd read to celebrate my 500th book on Goodreads: I enjoyed the entire story more this time around, with the wisdom and themes from Atticus bursting through each chapter. I look forward to my 3rd read in the future.

title: Perdido Street Station (New Crobuzon, #1)
author: China Miéville
review:
I don't know if I'm more impressed by the incredible imaginative designs produced by Melville's mind or the grim expression at the pointlessness of life.

5/5 worth being depressed from.

title: The Great Gatsby
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
review:
I found the enigmatic character of Gatsby fascinating, but I felt that while his wealth is eventually explained, his character was still largely mystifying, and that lack of explanation left me feeling unsatisfied. Near the end of the book the main character (who is surprisingly unimportant in the story, so perhaps I should just call him the narrator and observer) begins contemplating on how the tale of Gatsby and the debacle represents America, and how beautiful the rugged land of America is. This felt rather out of place and forced in, or perhaps I missed some connection with the rest of the book.

It's a short read and while worth reading it wouldn't be something I recommend you read with immediacy.

title: What We Owe the Future
author: William MacAskill
review:
As an AI researcher, I found the book "What We Owe the Future" to be a thought-provoking and insightful exploration of the ethical challenges that arise from our increasing technological capabilities and the potential impact on future generations.

The author offers a nuanced and interdisciplinary perspective on the complex issues surrounding the development and deployment of new technologies, and provides a surprisingly optimistic perspective on the survival of humanity. He emphasizes the importance of taking a long-term view of our actions and considering their impact on future generations.

MacAskill argues that we have a moral obligation to consider the interests of future generations in our decision-making and to act in ways that ensure the long-term sustainability of human society and the natural environment. 

I only wish more detail and discussion was provided, I feel that this is a just cause worthy of deeper exploration in all aspects touched upon.

title: Invisible Women: Data Bias in a World Designed for Men
author: Caroline Criado Pérez
review: The book I will recommend to anyone belittling the plight of women even in these modern times, and everyone else, too.

title: A Little Life
author: Hanya Yanagihara
review:
A Little Life is a harrowing, beautifully written meditation on trauma, love, and endurance. Yanagihara crafts a deeply immersive narrative, centering on Jude St. Francis’s lifelong struggle with unbearable pain and the friendships that try, but ultimately fail, to save him.

The novel’s prose is intimate - and I appreciate how trauma distorts the sense of time. While its relentless darkness may overwhelm some, its emotional truth is undeniable. This is not a story about healing but about the limits of love and survival. Not only are endings often not happy, but so, too, the whole tale can be tragic. 

Be Harold. Be Andy. Be Willem. And while you might not receive the love you want, love your Jude anyway.

title: Life of Pi
author: Yann Martel
review:
Bit of a mind dump:

A fantastic story with a compelling main character. To have a story with only one perspective and really the only human for large chunks of the tale, shows the incredibly writing ability by Martel. Having seen the film first, I'm happy to say that it is a good representation of the book. While some aspects are missed, the cinematic beauty seen in the film captures the essence of what the character experiences: the majesty of God. Philosophical, introspective and exploring new avenues of thought regarding the role of religion and the worship of deities, this is an excellent book. I cannot truly relate to Pi and his perspective on religion, but reading it through his perspective is fascinating; it does not sway me. I wouldn't consider it a proof of God(s), but the proof of the reality-altering ability the mind appears to have in situations with levels of stress impossible to process.

title: The Name of the Wind (The Kingkiller Chronicle, #1)
author: Patrick Rothfuss
review:
A masterful fantasy piece of writing. The only negative that must be mentioned is the lack of any female characters that are fully fleshed out. However, there was a great paragraph about the difficulties of being a working woman, the lack of opportunities and the dangers of a patriarchal society, which was insightful. I am told the next book improves with regard to representation of women, so I will continue on (without guilt). 

So quotable, expertly written. I thought I was getting sick of fantasy books due to overuse of tropes but the mastery of world building, character development and the well considered hard magic system Rothfuss presents here has pleasantly surprised me.

I'm itching to move onto the next one. Thank you, Mr. Rothfuss.
